text:
Channel estimation for multi-cell interference-limited networks is studied in this paper with a focus on the pilot contamination issue for both finite and large-scale MIMO systems. It is known that conventional channel estimation methods fail to differentiate between the desired and interfering signals due to the contamination of pilot signals when the same pilots are reused across different cells. As a result, this pilot contamination issue presents a fundamental performance barrier in multi-cell massive MIMO systems, and it does not diminish even when the number of antennas grows infinitely large.

We introduce a new approach to coordinated estimation based on the second order statistics of user channels, specifically their covariance. We show that using the structure of the channel, given by the fact that the angle-of-arrival (AOA) spread is finite, base stations can distinguish between useful and interfering signals even when they have the same pilot sequence. We prove that if the signal spaces of the channel covariances are disjoint, the pilot contamination effect reduces to zero in the large number of antennas regime.

Based on the conclusions in the previous section, we develop a low-rate coordination protocol based on a covariance-aware pilot assignment scheme. By wisely grouping users, such that the spatial orthogonality of their respective channel statistics is maximized, the corresponding CSI can be obtained with improved accuracy. The corresponding performance benefits in terms of CSI estimation accuracy and downlink achievable rates are demonstrated through simulations. It is shown that the proposed scheme can yield a substantial performance gain as compared with state-of-the-art schemes, even with moderate number of elements at the BS, and with some degree of element spacing.
